analogy: Think of "A Shirt in the Market" through the chapter's own opening images: 86 Social and Political Life A Shirt in the Market This chapter tells us the story of a shirt ! It begins with the production of cotton and ends with the sale of the shirt. We shall see that a chain of markets links the... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 86 Social and Political Life, 8 CHAPTER, 88 Social and Political Life, 90 Social and Political Life. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
A Shirt in the Market is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 86 Social and Political Life A Shirt in the Market This chapter tells us the story of a shirt !

The text explains that it begins with the production of cotton and ends with the sale of the shirt.

The text explains that we shall see that a chain of markets links the producer of cotton to the buyer of the shirt in the supermarket.

The text explains that buying and selling takes place at every step in the chain.

The text explains that 87 Did Swapna get a fair price on the cotton?

The text explains that where do you think large farmers would sell their cotton?

The text explains that how is their situation different from Swapna?

The text explains that a cotton farmer in Kurnool Swapna, a small farmer in Kurnool (Andhra Pradesh) grows cotton on her small piece of land.

The text explains that the bolls of the cotton plant are ripe and some have already burst, so Swapna is busy picking cotton.

The text explains that the bolls, which carry the cotton in them, do not burst open all at once so it takes several days to harvest the cotton.

The text explains that once the cotton is collected, instead of selling it at Kurnool cotton market, Swapna and her husband take the harvest to the local trader.

The text explains that at the beginning of the cropping season, Swapna had borrowed ` 2,500 from the trader at a very high interest rate to buy seeds, fertilisers, pesticides for cultivation.

The text explains that at that time, the local trader made Swapna agree to another condition.

The text explains that he made her promise to sell all her cotton to him.

The text explains that cultivation of cotton requires high levels of inputs such as fertilisers and pesticides and the farmers have to incur heavy expenses on account of these.

The text explains that most often, the small farmers need to borrow money to meet these expenses.

The text explains that putting-out system weavers producing cloth at home The merchant distributes work among the weavers based on the orders he has received for cloth.

The text explains that the weavers have no way of Chapter 8: A Shirt in the Market

The text explains that 90 Social and Political Life Weavers cooperative We have seen that the weavers are paid very little by the merchant under the putting out system.

The text explains that at times, the government helps the cooperatives by buying cloth from them at a reasonable price.

The text explains that for instance, the Tamil Nadu government runs a Free School Uniform programme in the state.The government procures the cloth for this programme from the powerloom weavers cooperatives.

The text explains that similarly, the government buys cloth from the handloom weavers cooperatives and sells it through stores known as Co-optex.

The text explains that the arrangement between the merchant and the weavers is an example of putting-out system, whereby the merchant supplies the raw material and receives the finished product.

The text explains that faced with such pressures from the buyers, the garment exporting factories, in turn, try to cut costs.

The text explains that what are the reasons that the businessperson is able to make a huge profit in the market?

The text explains that let us recall who were the people who were involved in this process of buying and selling.

The text explains that on the other hand, it is usually the rich and the powerful Chapter 8: A Shirt in the Market

The text explains that they have to depend for loans (as in the case of Swapna, the small farmer), for raw materials and marketing of their goods (weavers in the putting out system), and most often for employment (workers at the garment factory).

The text explains that american Museum of Natural History, Washington D.C.

The text explains that poster Women: A Visual History of the Womens Movement in India.

The text explains that the spellings of names in this map have been taken from various sources.

The text explains that 1999. Growth and Recession in Small-Scale Industry: A Study of Tamil Nadu Powerlooms Economic and Political Weekly.

The text explains that world Health Organization, Essential medicines and health products, http://www.who.int/medicines/services/inn/en/ www.cehat.org/rthc/policybrieffinal.pdf www.in.undp.org (Sustainable Development Goals  SDGs) www.infochangeindia.org Zubaan.

The text explains that the responsibility for the correctness of internal details rests with the publisher.

The text explains that the territorial waters of India extend into the sea to a distance of twelve nautical miles measured from the appropriate base line.

The text explains that the administrative headquarters of Chandigarh, Haryana and Punjab are at Chandigarh.

The text explains that the interstate boundaries amongst Arunachal Pradesh, Assam and Meghalaya shown on this map are as interpreted from the North Eastern Areas (Reorganisation) Act, 1971,but have yet to be verified.

The text explains that the external boundaries and coastlines of India agree with the Record / Master Copy certified by Survey of India.

The text explains that 6 The state boundaries between Uttarakhand & Uttar Pradesh, Bihar & Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh & Madhya Pradesh have not been verified by the Goverments concerned.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 8: A Shirt in the Market

prelims_traps: Do not treat 86 Social and Political Life and 8 CHAPTER as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to A Shirt in the Market; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain A Shirt in the Market through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 86 Social and Political Life and 8 CHAPTER to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of A Shirt in the Market, which statement is supported by the source text about 96 Social and Political Life?
options: The cloth market of Erode Erodes bi-weekly cloth market in Tamil Nadu is one of the largest cloth markets in the world.
Cloth that is made by weavers in the villages around is also brought here for sale.
Around the market are offices of cloth merchants who buy this cloth.
Other traders from many south Indian towns also come and purchase cloth in this market.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of A Shirt in the Market, which statement is supported by the source text about A Shirt in the Market?
options: On market days, you would also find weavers bringing cloth that has been made on order from the merchant.
These merchants supply cloth on order to garment manufacturers and exporters around the country.
They purchase the yarn and give instructions to the weavers about the kind of cloth that is to be made.
Over the years, these traders have developed extensive contacts with garment firms around the country from whom they get orders.

options: The weavers live in villages around and take the yarn supplied by these traders to their homes where the looms are located in sheds...

A variety of sarees, towels, shirting, ladies dress material and bedsheets are produced in these looms.
